vulnerability_type: Authentication bypass via flawed security-question verification logic (incorrectly implemented authentication algorithm)
description: AccountVerificationHelper.verifyAccount() validates the submitted security answers only inside containsKey() guards and otherwise falls through to 'return true', so an attacker-controlled request whose parameter names do not match the expected keys passes verification while still satisfying the answer-count check. The method also ignores the caller-supplied userId and always looks up a hard-coded account, so no answer is ever actually compared to the correct value unless the attacker chooses to send the expected key names.

vulnerability_type: Client-Side Enforcement of Server-Side Security (HTML form field restrictions not re-validated on the server)
description: The value constraints for the select, radio, checkbox, maxlength and readonly inputs exist only in the browser markup; the handler binds each @RequestParam directly and never re-validates that a submitted value belongs to the allowed set or respects the declared length/immutability. Any client that crafts the raw POST can supply arbitrary values for fields the UI presents as fixed or read-only.

vulnerability_type: Client-Side Enforcement of Server-Side Security / reliance on an untrusted client-supplied validation result
description: Format validation for the seven fields is performed by a JavaScript validate() function that writes its error count into a hidden 'error' form field, and the handler makes its accept/reject decision from that attacker-controlled parameter plus regex checks that are deliberately inverted (values matching the documented format are rejected). The server therefore trusts a validation verdict computed on the client instead of enforcing the format itself.

vulnerability_type: Exposure of sensitive information in a publicly served resource
description: The server-side secret PIN that forms part of the admin credential is written directly into the byte stream of a PNG image returned by an unauthenticated endpoint, so anyone who downloads the image can recover the secret. The credential check in the login endpoint reuses the same static PIN together with a fixed compiled-in password template, relying on obscurity rather than a real secret.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection
description: The login handler concatenates the request parameters username_login and password_login directly into the SQL string passed to Connection.prepareStatement, so the parameters are parsed as SQL rather than bound as values. An attacker controls the WHERE clause of the authentication query and can make it match without knowing the password.

vulnerability_type: Predictable password reset token (insufficiently random values)
description: The password reset token is derived from an unsalted MD5 of the attacker-supplied username, shuffled with java.util.Random whose seed is set to a constant derived from a static key when the username is the administrator. Because the PRNG is non-cryptographic and deterministically seeded, the reset token for a privileged account is fully reproducible offline, and the consuming reset endpoint accepts it without any additional authentication or expiry check.

vulnerability_type: Exposure of version-control repository / source code disclosure
description: An unauthenticated GET endpoint serves the application's version-control repository as a downloadable archive, disclosing historical source code including a previously committed hardcoded key. The same controller also embeds the administrator's valid reset-link value as a compiled-in constant, so leaking the repository leaks the token-generation logic needed to forge a privileged reset link.

vulnerability_type: Authentication/authorization bypass via HTTP verb tampering
description: The vote handler enforces its access control by comparing HttpServletRequest.getMethod() against a single literal value instead of checking the caller's identity or session. Any request that reaches the handler with a different verb (Spring also dispatches HEAD to @GetMapping handlers) skips the check entirely and mutates the vote state while returning the reward token in a response header.

vulnerability_type: Excessive data exposure / broken access control (sensitive employee data returned to unauthorized user, filtering left to the client)
description: The salaries endpoint builds a hard-coded XPath expression that selects UserID, FirstName, LastName, SSN and Salary for every Employee node in employees.xml, ignoring the caller's identity (the userId request parameter sent by the page is never read and no Managers/Manager restriction is applied). The full record set, including records of employees the authenticated user must not see, is serialized to JSON and the restriction to visible rows is performed only by the browser, so any client can read the complete data by calling the endpoint directly.

vulnerability_type: Exposure of sensitive business data through an API (privileged discount coupon disclosed by a list endpoint)
description: The unauthenticated coupon listing endpoint returns the entire coupon catalogue, and explicitly appends the internal 100%-discount super coupon to the response, so a secret that grants a free checkout is disclosed to any caller. The browser only ever needs a single code lookup, so the server hands out privileged data that the UI is expected to hide, and the checkout price is then computed client side from that response.

vulnerability_type: Use of broken cryptographic hash algorithm (MD5)
description: getMd5() builds a password digest with MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5") and returns the hex digest to the client. MD5 is a broken, collision-prone and extremely fast hash that is unsuitable for protecting secrets, so the original password is trivially recoverable from the published digest.

vulnerability_type: Password hashed with a fast one-way hash and no salt
description: The shared helper getHash(secret, algorithm) digests a password directly with MessageDigest without any salt, per-user randomness or key-stretching/work factor. Unsalted single-round digests are vulnerable to rainbow-table and precomputed dictionary lookups; the resulting digest is even returned to the client by the sha256 endpoint.

vulnerability_type: Use of cryptographically weak PRNG for security-relevant selection
description: Both hash endpoints pick the per-session secret with new java.util.Random().nextInt(...), a non-cryptographic linear congruential generator whose output is predictable from observed values or a guessed seed. Security-sensitive selection must use java.security.SecureRandom.

vulnerability_type: Use of cryptographically weak PRNG for security-relevant selection
description: getBasicAuth(HttpServletRequest) chooses the password that goes into the generated Basic-Auth credential using new java.util.Random().nextInt(...) instead of SecureRandom, so the credential material is drawn from a predictable, non-cryptographic generator.

vulnerability_type: Reversible encoding (Base64) used to protect credentials
description: getBasicAuth(username, password) concatenates the credentials and applies only Base64 encoding, and the GET endpoint then emits that value as an Authorization: Basic header to the caller. Base64 is a reversible transport encoding, not encryption, so the cleartext username and password are recoverable by anyone who sees the value.

vulnerability_type: Hard-coded credentials in source code
description: The public static SECRETS array embeds a fixed set of cleartext passwords in the source and exposes them to other classes, which then use them as the actual secret/credential material handed out by the hashing and Basic-Auth endpoints. Hard-coded secrets cannot be rotated and are readable by anyone with source or bytecode access.

vulnerability_type: Exposure of an RSA private key over an unprotected endpoint
description: getPrivateKey() generates an RSA key pair and returns the unencrypted PKCS#8 private key as a PEM block in the HTTP response body. The private key is never wrapped with a passphrase or kept server-side only, so any caller obtains full signing capability for the key pair used by the verification endpoint.

vulnerability_type: Inadequate encryption strength: RSA key generated with a very small public exponent
description: generateKeyPair() initializes the RSA KeyPairGenerator with an RSAKeyGenParameterSpec whose public exponent is randomly chosen from the small Fermat primes {3, 5, 17, 257, 65537}. Exponents such as 3 or 5 enable low-public-exponent attacks (e.g. Bleichenbacher-style signature forgery and broadcast/Coppersmith attacks) when padding is imperfect; the safe modern default of 65537 should be used unconditionally.

vulnerability_type: Weak/reversible obfuscation used to protect a stored database password
description: The lesson page embeds a database password stored in the WebSphere {xor} format, a fixed-key XOR-plus-Base64 obfuscation that is trivially reversible without any secret. Treating such an encoding as password protection means anyone able to read the configuration/template recovers the cleartext credential; the matching answer constant is also hard-coded in XOREncodingAssignment.

vulnerability_type: Cross-Site Request Forgery (state-changing POST protected only by a Referer header check)
description: The POST handler mutates server-side session state (userSessionData.setValue("csrf-get-success", ...)) with no synchronizer token or origin validation; its only defence is a string comparison of the Referer header against the Host header, which fails open when the Referer is absent and is trivially controlled by the requesting page. Any authenticated user's browser can be made to invoke the state change from a third-party origin.

vulnerability_type: Cross-Site Request Forgery with a hardcoded/static anti-CSRF token, state persisted before validation
description: createNewReview() writes the attacker-supplied review into the shared userReviews map keyed by the currently authenticated user before any anti-CSRF check runs, so the state change happens unconditionally. The only token is a single hardcoded constant (weakAntiCSRF) that is identical for every user and every session and is embedded in the page markup, so it provides no protection against a forged cross-origin form post.

vulnerability_type: Stored (persistent) Cross-Site Scripting via unescaped review content rendered into the DOM
description: Review text and star values submitted to POST /csrf/review are stored verbatim and returned by GET /csrf/review with no sanitisation or encoding; the client script substitutes them into an HTML template string and injects the result with jQuery append(), which parses and executes any markup contained in the stored content. Combined with the missing CSRF protection on the same endpoint, an attacker can plant the payload into another user's review list.

vulnerability_type: Cross-Site Request Forgery on a JSON API that relies on Content-Type instead of a token
description: The endpoint parses an arbitrary request body as JSON and acts on the caller's authenticated session with no synchronizer token, SameSite requirement or Origin/Referer enforcement; the implicit assumption that only same-origin XHR can send JSON is defeated because the handler happily processes a body sent with a simple content type from a plain cross-origin HTML form. The companion /csrf/feedback handler likewise performs no origin or token validation.

vulnerability_type: Login CSRF (unauthenticated registration endpoint that authenticates the victim's browser without CSRF protection)
description: The registration handler is permitAll and carries no CSRF token; it silently logs out whoever is currently authenticated and then calls request.login() with credentials taken straight from the posted form, establishing a new session cookie. A cross-origin auto-submitting form can therefore force a victim's browser into a session belonging to attacker-chosen credentials (login CSRF), after which the victim's activity is attributed to the attacker's account.

vulnerability_type: CSRF protection globally disabled in the Spring Security filter chain
description: The single SecurityFilterChain bean calls csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()) (and headers(...).disable()), removing the synchronizer-token filter for every request in the application including the form-login endpoint and every state-changing lesson POST. This configuration line is the shared root cause that makes all CSRF lesson endpoints, and the authentication flow itself, forgeable from any origin.

vulnerability_type: Information exposure through an error message (raw stack trace returned to the client)
description: When JSON deserialization of the request body fails, the handler serialises the full exception stack trace with ExceptionUtils.getStackTrace(e) into the response body returned to the caller, disclosing internal class names, library versions and code paths to an unauthenticated-controlled input path.

vulnerability_type: Deserialization of untrusted data (Java native deserialization / RCE)
description: The 'token' request parameter is Base64-decoded and fed straight into a plain java.io.ObjectInputStream whose readObject() is called with no class whitelist, ObjectInputFilter or type check before deserialization. Any Serializable class on the classpath (e.g. the bundled VulnerableTaskHolder gadget) is instantiated during readObject(), so attacker-controlled bytes trigger gadget side effects before the instanceof check is ever reached.

vulnerability_type: Deserialization gadget executing OS commands in readObject (command injection)
description: This Serializable class implements a custom private readObject() that, after defaultReadObject(), passes the deserialized taskAction field directly to Runtime.getRuntime().exec(). Because the field value comes from the attacker-supplied serialized stream, merely deserializing the object executes an OS command; the only guards are a 10-minute freshness window and a startsWith("sleep"/"ping") plus length check, which restrict but do not prevent attacker-chosen command execution.

vulnerability_type: Predictable session identifier / use of insufficiently random values for authentication token
description: The session identifier handed out as the 'hijack_cookie' authentication token is built by GENERATE_SESSION_ID as a monotonically incrementing static counter concatenated with the current epoch-millisecond timestamp, seeded once from the non-cryptographic java.util.Random. Because both components are sequential and observable, an attacker who obtains one cookie can enumerate identifiers issued to other users and authenticate as them, since authenticate() trusts any supplied id found in the session queue.

vulnerability_type: Client-side enforcement of server-side business logic (HTML/hidden field price tampering)
description: The checkout handler accepts both the quantity (QTY) and the order total (Total) as request parameters and parses them directly, trusting the price/total computed by client-side JavaScript in the lesson page. The unit price is never re-looked-up and the total is never recomputed server-side, so an assumed-immutable hidden form field controls the transaction amount.

vulnerability_type: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (unescaped reflection of request parameter into an HTML-rendered response)
description: The 'person' request parameter is placed verbatim (only string-reversed) into the AttackResult feedback message via feedbackArgs, and the framework applies only JSON escaping (AttackResult constructor) before returning it; the lesson front-end then strips those escapes and injects the message into the DOM with jQuery .html() in LessonContentView.js, so HTML/script markup supplied by the caller is parsed and executed instead of displayed as text. No HTML encoding or output sanitization is applied anywhere on this path.

vulnerability_type: Insecure Direct Object Reference (missing horizontal access control on profile read)
description: The GET handler takes the userId path variable straight from the client and instantiates UserProfile(userId), which resolves the record by that raw identifier, then returns the resolved profile. The only comparison against the session-bound 'idor-authenticated-user-id' is used to steer lesson feedback, never to deny access, so any authenticated user can read another user's profile record by supplying that user's identifier.

vulnerability_type: Insecure Direct Object Reference on write combined with mass assignment of a privilege attribute
description: The PUT handler loads the target record with UserProfile(userId) from the client-supplied path variable and then copies attacker-controlled fields from the deserialized request body, including the privilege-bearing 'role', onto that record before persisting it. There is no ownership or role check: the comparison with the session's authenticated user id only selects which lesson message to return, and because a null session id still satisfies the branch condition the update path is reachable even without completing the lesson login.

vulnerability_type: Use of hard-coded credentials in authentication check
description: The completed() handler authenticates by comparing the submitted username and password against string literals embedded directly in the Java source instead of validating against a credential store with salted password hashes. Anyone who can read the compiled class or source recovers a working account, and the credentials cannot be rotated or revoked.

vulnerability_type: Credentials embedded in client-side JavaScript and transmitted in cleartext
description: submit_secret_credentials() holds a username and password as hex-escaped string literals in a script served to every browser and POSTs them as an unprotected JSON body, so the secret is recoverable both by reading the delivered script and by passively observing the request. The hex escaping is only obfuscation and provides no confidentiality; the server-side /InsecureLogin/login handler accepts the request without any transport or payload protection.

vulnerability_type: JWT signature verification bypass (alg=none / unsigned token accepted)
description: The access_token cookie is parsed with Jwts.parser().setSigningKey(...).parse(token) instead of parseClaimsJws(token), so a token whose header declares no/none algorithm and carries an empty signature is accepted without any signature check. The resulting 'user' and 'admin' claims are then trusted for authorization, allowing privilege escalation to admin. The signing key is additionally a hardcoded dictionary word compiled into the class.

vulnerability_type: Weak/hardcoded HMAC signing key for JWT
description: Tokens issued and verified by this endpoint are signed with HS256 using a hardcoded secret picked at random from a five-element list of common English dictionary words. The key has far too little entropy for an HMAC secret, so an attacker holding any issued token can recover the key offline with a dictionary/brute-force attack and mint arbitrary tokens that pass verification.

vulnerability_type: JWT signature verification bypass (alg=none / unsigned token accepted)
description: The bearer token taken from the Authorization header is parsed with Jwts.parser().setSigningKey(...).parse(...) rather than parseClaimsJws(...), which accepts an unsigned token whose header algorithm is set to none. The 'user' claim from that unverified token is then used as the authenticated identity, so any user can be impersonated. The HMAC secret is also a hardcoded literal in the same class.

vulnerability_type: Refresh token not bound to user; expired access token accepted to mint new tokens
description: The token refresh handler takes the username from the supplied access token even when that token is expired (the ExpiredJwtException branch reads the claims anyway) and then only checks that the submitted refresh token exists in a single global list, without verifying it was issued to that same user. An attacker who obtains any leaked/expired access token can pair it with their own valid refresh token and receive a freshly signed access token for the victim's identity.

vulnerability_type: SQL injection via JWT 'kid' header in the signing key resolver
description: The custom SigningKeyResolver reads the attacker-controlled 'kid' header claim out of the unverified JWT header and concatenates it directly into a SELECT statement executed via Statement.executeQuery. Besides classic SQL injection, this lets the attacker make the query return a key value of their choosing, so they can sign a forged token that then passes verification and gain full authorization bypass.

vulnerability_type: Verification key fetched from attacker-controlled 'jku' header URL (untrusted JWKS)
description: The endpoint decodes the JWT without verification, reads the 'jku' header claim and builds a JwkProvider pointing at that URL with no allow-list or origin check, then verifies the token with whatever RSA public key is served there. An attacker can host their own JWKS, sign a token with the matching private key and have it accepted; the outbound fetch to an arbitrary URL is also a server-side request forgery vector.

vulnerability_type: Log Injection / Log Forging (improper output neutralization for logs)
description: The `username` request parameter is written into the simulated 'Login failed for username:' log entry without neutralizing line-break/CRLF characters; the handler merely rewrites newlines into markup instead of rejecting or encoding them, so an attacker controls the record boundaries and can forge additional log lines that appear to be genuine server events.

vulnerability_type: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting
description:
The handler echoes the attacker-supplied `username` back in the AttackResult output after only JSON-escaping it and blocking a couple of literal tag names, and even injects raw `
` markup into it. The client renders that output with jQuery `.html()`, so arbitrary HTML/script supplied in the parameter executes in the victim's browser.

vulnerability_type: Insertion of Sensitive Information (admin credential) into Log File
description: The component constructor writes the generated Admin account password to the application log at INFO level, protected only by Base64 encoding, which is reversible transport encoding rather than a confidentiality control. Anyone able to read the application log recovers valid administrator credentials that the same endpoint accepts for authentication.

vulnerability_type: Missing function-level access control (broken access control) exposing all user records
description: The listUsers() and usersService() GET handlers for /access-control/users return the full user list (each mapped to a DisplayUser containing username, admin flag, and a password-derived hash) without performing any authorization/role check, so any unauthenticated or non-admin caller can enumerate all accounts. The company's fixed sibling endpoint (usersFixed for /access-control/users-admin-fix) demonstrates the intended isAdmin() check.

vulnerability_type: Mass assignment / privilege escalation via unauthorized admin user creation
description: The addUser() POST handler binds the whole User request body (including the boolean admin field) via @RequestBody and persists it directly through the repository with no authorization check and no field allow-listing, so any caller can create a new account with admin privileges. Both the base and the 'admin-fix' path share this same unprotected handler, so no mitigated variant exists.

vulnerability_type: Exposure of password-derived hash using a predictable/static, short salt
description: genUserHash() computes a SHA-256 over password + a hardcoded static salt (PASSWORD_SALT_SIMPLE/ADMIN defined in MissingFunctionAC) + username, and DisplayUser serializes this userHash field into the API response. Because the salt is constant, short, and predictable and the hash is exposed to clients, the derived secret can be reconstructed/verified offline; both the base and 'admin-fix' salts are static so neither value is truly protected.

vulnerability_type: Open Redirect (URL Redirection to Untrusted Site)
description: The GET handler takes the user-controlled 'url' request parameter and builds a Spring redirect view via string concatenation ('redirect:' + url) with no validation or allow-listing of the destination host, producing an HTTP 302 to any attacker-supplied external site.

vulnerability_type: Host header poisoning in password reset link
description: The password reset e-mail's link is built from the attacker-controlled HTTP Host header (request.getHeader(HttpHeaders.HOST)) which is interpolated into the reset-link template without validation. An attacker can supply a host they control so the victim's one-time reset token is delivered to the attacker, enabling account takeover. The generated token is stored in a shared static store (ResetLinkAssignment.resetLinks) consumed by the reset/change-password endpoints.

vulnerability_type: Weak/guessable security-question password recovery with no lockout
description: The 'forgot password' flow verifies identity solely via a security-question answer drawn from a tiny, easily-guessable fixed set (hardcoded COLORS map), with no rate limiting or lockout. An attacker can brute-force the answer for other users (tom, admin, larry) and take over their accounts.

vulnerability_type: Account/username enumeration via observable response discrepancy
description: The password-reset endpoint returns distinguishable responses for valid versus non-existent usernames ('User {username} is not a valid user' vs. a generic wrong-answer failure), letting an attacker enumerate which accounts exist for use in phishing or targeted attacks.

vulnerability_type: Predictable generated reset password
description: The reset flow e-mails a new password that is deterministically derived from the account username (StringUtils.reverse(username)), and the login endpoint accepts that same reversed-username value as the password. Because the recovery credential is fully predictable from the public username, anyone can compute it.

vulnerability_type: Path Traversal via file upload (arbitrary file write)
description: The shared base method execute() builds the destination path with new File(uploadDirectory, fullName) and writes the uploaded bytes to it without validating or canonicalizing fullName. ProfileUpload passes the user-supplied fullName request parameter straight through, so an attacker can traverse out of the upload directory and write/overwrite arbitrary files.

vulnerability_type: Path Traversal via file upload with bypassable sanitization (arbitrary file write)
description: ProfileUploadFix attempts to sanitize the filename with a single non-recursive fullName.replace("../", "") before delegating to the base execute() sink (new File(uploadDirectory, fullName)). The one-pass replace is trivially bypassable (e.g. nested sequences that collapse back into ../ after one removal), so arbitrary file write outside the upload directory is still possible.

vulnerability_type: Path Traversal via multipart filename (arbitrary file write)
description: ProfileUploadRemoveUserInput removes the fullName form field but instead passes file.getOriginalFilename() into the base execute() sink (new File(uploadDirectory, filename)). The multipart Content-Disposition filename is fully attacker-controlled (e.g. via an intercepting proxy) and is not validated or canonicalized, so directory traversal and arbitrary file write remain exploitable.

vulnerability_type: Zip Slip - Path Traversal via archive extraction (arbitrary file write)
description: processZipUpload() iterates zip entries and resolves each output path with new File(tmpZipDirectory.toFile(), e.getName()) then Files.copy() into it, using the archive-supplied entry name without validating that it stays within the extraction directory. A crafted entry name containing traversal sequences lets the extracted content be written outside the target directory, overwriting arbitrary files.

vulnerability_type: Path Traversal via request parameter (arbitrary file read)
description: getProfilePicture() builds new File(catPicturesDirectory, id + ".jpg") from the request-supplied id parameter and returns the file contents. The only guard rejects the raw query string when it literally contains '..' or '/', but request.getParameter() URL-decodes the value, so URL-encoded traversal sequences bypass the filter and allow reading files outside the intended directory.

vulnerability_type: Use of default/hardcoded credentials
description: The login endpoint authenticates against hardcoded factory default credentials (constants DEFAULT_USERNAME/DEFAULT_PASSWORD both set to 'admin') that were never removed before shipping, allowing trivial account takeover of the staging/production admin console.

vulnerability_type: Sensitive information disclosure via verbose error / debug output
description: With debug mode left enabled the trigger endpoint returns a full verbose stack trace in the HTTP response body that embeds environment variables and secrets (DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD and the SYSTEM_API_TOKEN), leaking sensitive configuration to any unauthenticated caller.

vulnerability_type: Exposure of sensitive information via unauthenticated actuator endpoint
description: The simulated actuator/env endpoint returns internal configuration as JSON without any authentication, disclosing a secret (systemApiKey) and internal profile/feature data to any anonymous caller because sensitive management endpoints were left open.

vulnerability_type: Forgeable authentication cookie (reversible encoding used as auth token)
description: The authentication cookie value is produced by EncDec.encode via a fully reversible transformation (lowercased username concatenated with a static SALT, string-reversed, hex-encoded, then base64-encoded) with no cryptographic signature, HMAC, or secret key. Because decode is the exact inverse and the scheme is deducible from an issued cookie, an attacker can craft a valid cookie for any known username; cookieLoginFlow decodes and trusts that username to authenticate, allowing authentication bypass and impersonation.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (arbitrary query execution)
description: The `query` request parameter is passed unmodified to Statement.executeQuery(), so the entire SQL statement is attacker-controlled. No parameterization or validation is applied before execution.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (arbitrary DML execution)
description: The `query` request parameter is passed directly to Statement.executeUpdate(), allowing an attacker to run arbitrary data-modifying SQL (e.g. UPDATE) with no parameterization.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (arbitrary DDL execution)
description: The `query` request parameter is passed directly to Statement.executeUpdate(), allowing arbitrary schema-altering SQL (e.g. ALTER TABLE) to be executed without parameterization.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (arbitrary query execution / privilege escalation)
description: The `query` request parameter is executed as-is via Statement.executeQuery(), letting an attacker run arbitrary SQL including GRANT statements to escalate database privileges. No parameterization is applied.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (string concatenation in WHERE clause)
description: The `account`, `operator` and `injection` request parameters are concatenated into a SELECT string that is executed with Statement.executeQuery(), allowing tautology-based injection into the last_name filter.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (concatenation into prepared-statement text)
description: Although login_count is bound as a parameter, the `userid` request parameter is concatenated directly into the query string before it is handed to prepareStatement(), leaving the userid portion of the WHERE clause injectable.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (string concatenation, confidentiality breach)
description: The `name` and `auth_tan` request parameters are concatenated into a SELECT executed with Statement.executeQuery(). The static log() helper additionally concatenates the same action string into an INSERT executed via executeUpdate(), giving a second injectable sink in this file.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (string concatenation, integrity breach)
description: The `name` and `auth_tan` request parameters are concatenated into a SELECT string that is run with Statement.execute(), permitting injection that modifies data (e.g. updating salaries). No parameterization is used.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (string concatenation in LIKE clause, availability)
description: The `action_string` request parameter is concatenated into the LIKE clause of a SELECT executed with Statement.executeQuery(), enabling injection that can drop/manipulate the access_log table (availability attack).

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (UNION-based, string concatenation)
description: The account name (last_name) is concatenated into a SELECT executed with Statement.executeQuery() in injectableQuery(), enabling UNION-based extraction from other tables. The same sink is reached by the two input-validation 'mitigation' controllers whose blocklists (reject-space, strip FROM/SELECT) are incomplete and therefore bypassable.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (string concatenation, user-enumeration/registration)
description: In registerNewUser() the `username_reg` request parameter is concatenated into the checkUserQuery string executed with Statement.executeQuery(), allowing injection (e.g. to alter existing rows / bypass the uniqueness check). The subsequent INSERT correctly uses a PreparedStatement, but the pre-check query is the injectable sink.

vulnerability_type: SQL Injection (ORDER BY clause injection despite prepared statement)
description: The `column` request parameter is concatenated into the ORDER BY clause of the query passed to prepareStatement(). Because parameter binding cannot parameterize an ORDER BY identifier, the prepared statement provides no protection and the sort column remains injectable (blind/order-by injection).

vulnerability_type: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: The furBall method constructs a java.net.URL directly from the user-supplied 'url' request parameter and calls openStream() on it, issuing a server-side outbound HTTP request to an attacker-influenced destination. This is a genuine SSRF sink where user input reaches an outbound network fetch.

vulnerability_type: Insecure Deserialization via known-vulnerable component (XStream RCE)
description: The attack1 endpoint feeds the attacker-controlled 'payload' request parameter directly to xstream.fromXML() (VulnerableComponentsLesson.java line 42) using XStream 1.4.5 (pom.xml xstream.version=1.4.5), a version vulnerable to CVE-2013-7285. XStream has no type filtering/whitelist configured, so a crafted XML document can instantiate arbitrary classes during deserialization, enabling remote code execution. The only input sanitization strips whitespace/plus characters and does not prevent the exploit.

vulnerability_type: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting
description: The credit-card parameter field1 is concatenated directly into the shopping-cart HTML string (cart.append("..." + field1 + ...)) and returned as the AttackResult output without any HTML encoding. The client renders this via LessonContentView.renderOutput() using jQuery .html(), so an injected script in field1 executes in the victim's browser. Only field2 is filtered against the XSS pattern; field1 is not.

vulnerability_type: DOM-based Cross-Site Scripting
description: The client-side Backbone route 'test/:param' passes the URL fragment (param) unmodified through LessonController.testHandler() to LessonContentView.showTestParam(), which injects it into the DOM via $('.lesson-content').html('test:' + param). Because .html() parses HTML, attacker-controlled markup in the location hash executes in the browser. The server endpoints only verify exploitation (the injected script calls webgoat.customjs.phoneHome which posts to phone-home-xss).

vulnerability_type: Stored Cross-Site Scripting
description: Comment text posted to POST /CrossSiteScriptingStored/stored-xss is persisted server-side (StoredXssComments) and returned verbatim by the GET endpoint. The render sink in stored-xss.js substitutes the raw comment text into an HTML template (html.replace('COMMENT', result[i].text)) and inserts it with $('#list').append(comment); jQuery parses the string as HTML, so a stored script payload executes for every user who views the comment feed. No output encoding or sanitization is applied on store or render.

vulnerability_type: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection
description: The shared parseXml helper in CommentsCache builds an XMLInputFactory via XMLInputFactory.newInstance() and only sets ACCESS_EXTERNAL_DTD/ACCESS_EXTERNAL_SCHEMA to empty when its securityEnabled flag is true. All three assignment controllers (SimpleXXE, ContentTypeAssignment, BlindSendFileAssignment) invoke parseXml with securityEnabled=false and pass the attacker-supplied request body straight to createXMLStreamReader/unmarshal, so external DTDs and entities are resolved. This enables local file disclosure (simple), file exfiltration to an out-of-band server (blind), and the same attack via an XML Content-Type header (content-type bypass).
